us-politics.headline: US Rescues Downed Airman From Iran, Trump Threatens Strikes
us-politics.body:
The US military rescued a service member shot down over Iran, with the CIA running a deception campaign to support the operation. Trump announced the rescue on Easter Sunday, framing it as a demonstration of American resolve in the ongoing conflict.

Trump also issued a profane threat on social media, warning Iran that the US would strike power plants and bridges if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened. The waterway is critical to global oil flows, and its closure has already sent jet fuel costs surging, hitting airlines and travelers hard.

A retired Army general predicted the war would conclude in under 60 days. The former World Bank chief warned Iran's economy is collapsing toward a barter system under the pressure of the conflict and sanctions.

sources.title: Inside the daring rescue of airman behind enemy lines: How CIA assisted with 'deception campaign'
Trump threatens Iran with ‘Hell’ over Strait of Hormuz in profane post
Trump drops Easter Sunday f-bomb in new threat to Iran
Trump vows US will strike Iran’s power plants, bridges if Strait of Hormuz is not reopened
US rescues missing service member from downed jet in Iran: Trump
Retired Army general predicts Iran war wrapped up in ‘less than 60 days’
Former World Bank chief says Iran economy collapsing into ‘barter system’ amid conflict
Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: Trump Fires Bondi, Faces Pushback on Emergency Powers and Elections
us-politics.body:
Trump fired Attorney General Pam Bondi, a move NPR reports comes alongside signs he is retreating from some campaign commitments. The dismissal adds to a pattern of executive turbulence that critics across the spectrum are watching closely.

A federal judge blocked the administration's push to collect race-based admissions data from universities. Separately, a new ad campaign backed by Republicans is pushing back against Trump's stated interest in federalizing election administration, a rare instance of intra-party public opposition.

Commentators at The Hill argue Trump's use of emergency powers has itself become the emergency, citing broad invocations of executive authority. Supporters counter that strong executive action is necessary given the scale of challenges the administration faces at home and abroad.

sources.title: Politics chat: Trump fires Attorney General Bondi, seeks to break campaign promises
Trump’s abuse of emergency powers is the real national emergency
Federal judge blocks Trump push to collect race-based admissions data
New ad campaign speaks out against Trump’s threat to ‘federalize’ elections

us-politics.headline: Pope Leo Calls for Peace; NATO Fractures Over Spain Airspace Row
us-politics.body:
Pope Leo used his Easter address to deliver a pointed call for peace, with the Washington Post noting the message landed squarely against the backdrop of the US-Iran conflict and broader global instability. The speech drew wide attention given the timing.

Meanwhile, Spain's decision to ban US military aircraft from its airspace is prompting analysts to question the durability of NATO as a universal alliance. The Hill describes it as a sign of a 'post-global' era in which shared alliances no longer guarantee shared obligations. Trump and Vice President Vance are also backing Viktor Orban in Hungary's upcoming election, a stance that further strains relations with European partners who view Orban as a democratic backslider.

sources.title: On Easter, Pope Leo delivers commanding message of peace to a world at war
The ‘post-global’ NATO: Spain’s airspace ban signals the death of universal alliances
In Hungarian election, Trump and Putin are backing Viktor Orban

us-politics.headline: Harris Eyes 2028; White House Pledges Permanent Moon Presence
us-politics.body:
Fox News reports that Kamala Harris's recent travel schedule and public comments point clearly toward a 2028 presidential run, though Harris has made no formal announcement. Her appearances have grown more frequent and more pointed since the 2024 loss.

On a different front, the White House signaled a firm commitment to the Artemis program, vowing the US will not cede ground on the moon to rival powers. Officials framed a permanent lunar base as both a scientific and strategic priority, with China's own moon ambitions serving as the backdrop.

sources.title: The White House vows America will never give up the moon again
Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that fuel supplies will continue normally, as Prime Minister Albanese works to contain a growing domestic shortage. The assurances follow diplomatic outreach to key regional partners, with Japan's government among those offering direct guarantees.

At home, NSW authorities fined nearly 100 service stations $1,100 each for displaying misleading petrol prices during the crunch. The crackdown signals regulators are watching retail behaviour closely as supply anxiety spreads.

Farmers are feeling the pressure hardest. Some say rising fuel costs have made it difficult to cover basic expenses, with one describing the situation as "struggling massively." The crisis cuts across city and country, and the government faces pressure to show it has the situation under control before it worsens.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel

au-politics.headline: Police negligence lawsuit looms after officer killed by Dezi Freeman
au-politics.body:
The sisters of a police officer killed by Dezi Freeman are preparing to sue NSW Police for negligence, according to their lawyer. The case centres on whether police failed in their duty of care in the lead-up to the fatal incident.

The lawsuit raises uncomfortable questions about internal police decision-making and risk management. If it proceeds, it could expose operational failures and set a precedent for how courts assess police liability when officers are harmed on duty.

Police have not publicly responded to the planned legal action. The case is likely to draw significant attention given its intersection of workplace safety, institutional accountability, and the circumstances surrounding Freeman's actions.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says

au-politics.headline: US airman rescued from Iran as nuclear diplomacy stirs
au-politics.body:
US special forces rescued an F-15 airman from Iran in a covert operation, the details of which remain limited. The rescue adds tension to an already volatile diplomatic picture between Washington and Tehran.

Separately, Iran has signalled a willingness to negotiate with the Trump administration, with commentary from the right describing the move as an olive branch. Whether the rescue operation complicates or runs parallel to those diplomatic overtures is unclear, and neither government has directly addressed the overlap.

The two developments together illustrate the contradictory pressures shaping US-Iran relations - back-channel diplomacy on one track, military operations on another.

sources.title: Iran has offered Trump an olive branch
US F-15 airman rescued in Iran after special forces operation

au-politics.headline: Easter weekend: tragedy, celebration, and sport across Australia
au-politics.body:
A seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east on Easter Sunday, one of several crash fatalities reported over the long weekend. The incident is under investigation.

Across the country, Australians marked Easter with community events and egg hunts, with ABC reporting a broadly upbeat public mood despite global economic uncertainty. Tim Tszyu added to the weekend's headlines by dominating Denis Nurja in Wollongong to claim a boxing title, delivering a commanding performance in front of a home crowd.

In sport, the Western Bulldogs beat Essendon in AFL despite losing two more players to injury, the Sydney Kings claimed the NBL championship in overtime after Adelaide blew a late lead, and the Canberra Raiders suffered a heavy NRL loss to the Newcastle Knights.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Tim Tszyu dominates Denis Nurja to claim title in Wollongong
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Bulldogs down plucky Bombers but lose two more players to injury
Raiders' season lurches towards disaster as Knights run riot
Kings break NBL trophy after dramatic overtime win as 36ers blow late lead

world.headline: US-Iran War Enters Critical Phase as Gulf Feels the Burn
world.body:
Now 37 days in, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran is reshaping the entire Gulf region. Iran has struck Kuwait's power and water infrastructure, closed the Strait of Hormuz to international shipping, and downed at least two US F-15E fighters - though both pilots were rescued inside Iranian territory, with Trump calling the second recovery 'miraculous'.

The Hormuz closure is sending shockwaves through Europe and beyond. Nepal has moved to two-day weekends to conserve fuel, gas prices are surging in the US, and European leaders are scrambling. Italy's prime minister called the Gulf 'fundamental' to European security. Trump has offered no concrete plan to reopen the strait.

Diplomacy is flickering. Oman is mediating transit talks with Iran, and former Iranian foreign minister Zarif has floated a peace roadmap - though Gulf states say trust has collapsed. Polls show the war is deeply unpopular with the American public.

sources.title: Why an attack on Bushehr nuclear plant would be catastrophic for the Gulf
Nepal announces two-day weekends as fuel crisis caused by Iran war deepens
Trump offers no concrete plan as Strait of Hormuz closure shocks Europe
Iran’s ex-FM Zarif proposes peace roadmap; Gulf points at erosion of trust
Italian PM says the Gulf is ‘fundamental’ for Europe’s security
Oman, Iran discuss smooth transit in Strait of Hormuz, Muscat says
“We blame Trump”: Drivers line up for free gas as prices surge in the US
US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states
US airman injured but safe after rescue from inside Iran, Trump says
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran

world.headline: Houthis Strike Ben Gurion; Israeli Forces Filmed Attacking Mosque
world.body:
Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile attack on Israel's Ben Gurion airport, escalating their campaign against Israeli targets. The strike marks one of the most direct Houthi hits on Israeli civilian infrastructure to date.

Separately, footage circulating online shows an Israeli military dog attacking a Palestinian man inside a mosque, drawing sharp international condemnation. Palestinian Christians are marking a subdued Holy Week under heavy Israeli movement restrictions in the West Bank and Jerusalem. An Al Jazeera opinion piece argues Israel's sustained campaign against UNRWA is a deliberate strategy to dismantle Palestinian civilian support structures.

sources.title: Footage shows Israeli military dog attacking Palestinian man inside mosque
Israel’s attack on UNRWA is central to its genocide of the Palestinians
Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week
Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport

world.headline: Pope Leo Urges Peace; Canada Heads to the Moon
world.body:
Newly elected Pope Leo used his first Easter Mass to call on world leaders to choose peace, delivering the message as multiple armed conflicts rage across the Middle East and Eastern Europe. The address carried particular weight given the scale of ongoing violence in Gaza, Iran, and Ukraine.

On a brighter note, Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen has been confirmed as the first Canadian to travel to the moon, joining NASA's Artemis lunar program. In Ukraine, drone strikes hit a Russian oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod and a port in Primorsk, continuing Kyiv's campaign against Russian energy infrastructure.

sources.title: Pope Leo calls for global leaders to choose peace in his first Easter Mass
Jeremy Hansen is the first Canadian to head to the moon
Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

world.headline: Measles Kills Nearly 100 Children in Bangladesh
world.body:
A suspected measles outbreak has killed close to 100 children in Bangladesh, alarming health officials across South Asia. The outbreak points to gaps in vaccination coverage, likely worsened by disruptions to routine immunisation programmes in recent years.

Authorities are racing to contain the spread, but the scale of child deaths has already prompted calls for an emergency public health response. The outbreak is one of the deadliest measles events in the region in recent memory.

sources.title: Suspected measles outbreak kills nearly 100 children in Bangladesh

ai.headline: AI Creeps Into Maps, Music, and Your Daily Routine
ai.body:
Google's Gemini integration in Maps is now planning full itineraries, and a hands-on test found it genuinely useful rather than gimmicky. Meanwhile, Japan is deploying physical AI robots to fill labor shortages in jobs that go unfilled, not to displace workers who exist. The contrast matters: AI is landing softest where it solves a concrete, unglamorous problem.

Not everyone is benefiting. A folk musician found herself targeted by AI-generated fakes of her voice, then hit with copyright claims from a troll exploiting the resulting confusion. The story illustrates a pattern where AI creates the harm and existing legal frameworks fail to address it cleanly. Karpathy's publicly shared LLM idea file adds a quieter note: even leading researchers are still figuring out how to think with these tools.

sources.title: I let Gemini in Google Maps plan my day and it went surprisingly well
In Japan, the robot isn’t coming for your job; it’s filling the one nobody wants
A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
LLM Wiki – example of an "idea file"

ai.headline: Always-On AI Glasses Draw Backlash, Grammarly Stumbles
ai.body:
A new campaign called Banray is pushing back hard against always-on AI glasses, arguing the devices normalize ambient surveillance in public spaces. The site frames the technology not as a convenience but as a social threat. It arrives as wearable AI hardware accelerates toward mainstream release.

Grammarly, meanwhile, is facing scrutiny over what The Verge calls its 'sloppelganger saga' - a pattern where the AI writing tool introduces errors rather than catching them. One developer spent eight years wanting to build a personal project and finished it in three months using AI assistance, a sharp counterpoint that shows the technology's uneven performance depending on task type.

sources.title: Banray.eu: Raising awareness of the terrible idea that is always-on AI glasses
Grammarly’s sloppelganger saga
Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI

ai.headline: Security Failures Hit BrowserStack, CBP, and Google Workspace
ai.body:
BrowserStack appears to be leaking user email addresses, with evidence pointing to an internal source rather than an external breach. Separately, sensitive CBP facility codes surfaced in online flashcard sets, likely uploaded by trainees who didn't realize the material was restricted. Both cases show that data exposure often comes from mundane operational failures, not sophisticated attacks.

Google Workspace users got a reminder of a different risk: account suspensions with little recourse. One detailed account describes losing access to years of business data with no clear path to appeal. Platform dependency at scale continues to carry real, underappreciated costs.

sources.title: Someone at BrowserStack Is Leaking Users' Email Address
CBP facility codes sure seem to have leaked via online flashcards
Sad Story of My Google Workspace Account Suspension

ai.headline: Artemis II Flies On; Microsoft's Copilot Branding Is a Mess
ai.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew caught their first view of the Moon's far side, and the mission is going smoothly enough that the biggest news is a frozen urine management issue. That's a good problem to have. The mission marks the first crewed lunar flyby in over fifty years.

Back on the ground, a breakdown of Microsoft's product naming finds the company has applied the 'Copilot' label to a sprawling, inconsistent range of products. The analysis counts enough distinct Copilot-branded offerings to suggest the name has lost any specific meaning. It reads less like a strategy and more like a branding accident that compounded over time.

sources.title: Artemis II crew see first glimpse of far side of Moon
Artemis II is going so well that we're left to talk about frozen urine
How many products does Microsoft have named 'Copilot'?

btc.headline: North Korea Drained $270M From Drift in Six-Month Operation
btc.body:
Drift Protocol has confirmed that a $270-280 million exploit was the work of North Korean state-linked hackers who spent six months embedding themselves inside the project before striking. The attack was not opportunistic. Investigators describe months of deliberate preparation, suggesting the operation involved intelligence tradecraft rather than simple code vulnerabilities.

The breach is one of the largest DeFi exploits on record and raises hard questions about how nation-state actors are targeting crypto infrastructure. North Korea has a well-documented history of using crypto theft to fund its weapons programs, and the Drift case shows the patience and sophistication those operations now involve.

sources.title: Drift says $270 million exploit was a six-month North Korean intelligence operation
Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: AI and Quantum Threats Are Rewriting Crypto's Security Calculus
btc.body:
Ledger's CTO warned this week that AI is actively worsening crypto's security landscape, making phishing attacks more convincing and exploit development faster. The warning lands as Bitcoin developers race separately to quantum-proof a $1.3 trillion network before quantum computing matures enough to crack existing cryptography.

Ant Group's blockchain arm added another dimension by launching a platform that lets AI agents transact autonomously on crypto rails - a capability that expands the attack surface further. The convergence of AI and crypto is accelerating on both sides of the security equation. Defenders are scrambling while the tools available to attackers keep improving.

sources.title: AI is making crypto's security problem even worse, Ledger CTO warns
Ant Group’s blockchain arm unveils platform for AI agents to transact on crypto rails
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Bitcoin Stalls at $67K as Bearish Sentiment Hits Multi-Week High
btc.body:
Bitcoin is stuck near $67,000 and the mood around it is souring fast. Bearish social media chatter hit a five-week high according to Santiment, while separate sentiment data shows BTC entering April at its most negative reading since the Iran war began. At least one trader is calling new price lows a matter of time.

The broader token market is adding to the gloom. A CoinTelegraph analysis argues crypto faces an existential supply problem - new tokens are being minted faster than genuine value is being created, diluting the ecosystem. Robert Kiyosaki, meanwhile, is using the uncertainty to push his familiar case for Bitcoin and gold as hard assets, drawing parallels to the 1974 breakdown of dollar-gold convertibility.

sources.title: Bitcoin holds steady as sentiment hits worst levels since Iran war began
New Bitcoin price lows 'matter of time' says trader with BTC stuck at $67K
Crypto faces ‘existential’ token problem as supply outpaces value creation
Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Robert Kiyosaki recommends Bitcoin, gold as 1974 shift comes full circle

food.name: Manti
food.description: Tiny hand-pinched dumplings filled with spiced lamb, drowned in garlicky yogurt and chilli butter
food.tip: Make the dumplings thumb-nail small — the ratio of dough to filling is what makes them special, so resist the urge to go bigger

exercise.name: Deck of Cards Workout
exercise.equipment: One standard deck of playing cards, exercise mat
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight move to each suit, flip cards one by one, and perform the reps shown — the randomness kills boredom and guarantees an unpredictable full-body session

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a sharp question about whether you are the same person you were ten years ago.
learn.why: On a slow Sunday it's the perfect rabbit hole because it branches naturally into Derek Parfit's work on personal identity, Buddhist concepts of the self, and even modern debates about consciousness — one hour in and you'll be questioning something you've never thought to question before
